title: Compara
overview: Collaborated to design and develop an MVP of a multimedia comparison tool, which allows researchers to create and publish studies.
process:
Compara was an MVP built by a team of four with input from a professor, who fulfilled the role of product owner. The brief was to create a platform for researches to conduct comparative research between artifacts—be it images, text, audio or video—and ability to analyze and download participants' answers in a JSON and CSV format. Existing solutions like Google Forms and Nettskjema was not satisfactory, due to it being difficult to compare multiple artifacts for different question types. My main work included leading the design, setting up database schemas, creating study and participant session endpoints, processing and visualizing participant data, and handling Docker deployment. I also created UI components for the frontend.
Following a discussion meeting and additional research, we developed a prototype in Figma, refined it through feedback, and then began setting up the project. We decided on a single-page application (SPA) using Vue on the frontend, and Nuxt on the backend with MongoDB to handle server-side logic, API endpoints, routing, and data storage within the same environment. For authentication, we implemented JWT for secure login and session management.
This was likely the most significant learning experience of my education so far. I had the opportunity to develop a project for a fictive client while working in a team, where we were responsible for organizing and scheduling the development ourselves. Additionally, I did end-to-end testing and setting up a basic CI/CD pipeline—that deployed our Docker image to the school server—for the first time. However, it did come with multiple challenges, and if I were to do it again, I would focus on designing a more scalable structure from the start.
info: Dashboard displaying all studies (draft, ongoing, and completed), with options to edit, duplicate, or delete each study.

title: Worldly
overview: A WoT project using a gyroscope-equipped physical globe to discover inventions, facts, and AI-generated quizzes about countries around the world.
process:
As the main web developer, I built the frontend, backend, and a custom country data API, connecting everything to Node-RED via Socket.io to visualize different user flows. The biggest challenge was coordinating the client, server, and Node-RED interface, managing loading states and receiving stable gyroscope coordinates from a teammate's sensor scripts.
Worldly started as a learning tool aimed at children, but expanded to target all ages. We moved away from showing random artworks, instead focusing on inventions, quizzes, and country facts, where the link to geography and learning outcomes is much clearer. The app uses the Google Maps API to translate gyroscope coordinates into countries, OpenAI to generate country-specific quizzes, and our own JSON-based API covering inventions and facts for 100 countries. Creating an interface for adding information and images for our API would be further work.

title: Twitter Clone
overview: A Twitter-like web application with a implemented RESTful API for managing users, posts, follows, and search, while incorporating basic caching and load balancing.
process:
In this project, a classmate and I built a small-scale clone of old Twitter. The site includes login/signup, a general feed, a search for tweets, hashtags, and users, and a profile page showing tweets, replies, and account information. The application was initially deployed on Render, but we later containerized it with Docker for easier local development. Our goal was to make it with performance and scalability in mind, even though we might be the only ones that signed up.
At the beginning of the project, we defined all the endpoints that were necessary for our application, and did our best to keep those RESTful. The backend was built with Python and FastAPI, using Pydantic to ensure consistent input and output. PostgreSQL suited the relational structure of Twitter-style data, while TypeScript with React on the frontend helped catch errors early and keep the code maintainable.
To handle potential high traffic, we set up three containers with Nginx as a load balancer to distribute requests efficiently. We implemented a simplified like batcher to reduce repeated database calls, and a basic caching system that tracked the last time a tweet was updated using a list of key-value pairs. We also did some unit and load testing to make sure everything worked as expected, although testing should've been integrated earlier in the process.
info: Create an account.
A home feed that showcases all tweets, where you can like and reply to others' tweets, and tweet yourself.
Reply to a tweet, with ability to edit or delete in case you typed something wrong.
Look at others' profiles, which shows general info about the user, tweets, replies, and following/followers.
Search tweets by hashtags.

title: Roots of Life
overview: A complete interactive story told through scrolling and animations highlighting UN Goal 15: Life on Land, focusing on deforestation.
process: This project was a collaborative effort with two classmates. My primary contribution was designing the storyboards and illustrating all the forest scenes in Adobe Illustrator (except for the background of the green hills), including the various elements and animations. For the final version of the project, where each student individually finalized their three projects over the course, I decided to transition most of the JavaScript to GSAP due to performance issues with the former.
